contraventionDescription: Parked in a residents or shared use parking place without clearly displaying either a permit or voucher or pay and display ticket issued for that place – Contravention Code 12

sections.heading: Parked in a residents or shared use parking place without clearly displaying either a permit or voucher or pay and display ticket issued for that place – Contravention Code 12
sections.content:
12R means no residents permit and 12T means no voucher or pay and display ticket.
If you have received a penalty charge notice for this contravention but parked in a shared use bay and failed to display a pay and display ticket then you should also look at contravention code 06.
sections.contentHtml:
12R means no residents permit and 12T means no voucher or pay and display ticket.

If you have received a penalty charge notice for this contravention but parked in a shared use bay and failed to display a pay and display ticket then you should also look at contravention code 06.

sections.heading: How to have your penalty charge notice (PCN) for this parking contravention cancelled.
sections.content: We may be able to assist you in having your penalty charge notice for this contravention cancelled.
sections.contentHtml: We may be able to assist you in having your penalty charge notice for this contravention cancelled.

sections.heading: Follow our 3 stage process:
sections.content:
1 Check: Specific grounds for appeal for this contravention (below on this page)
This may also include diagrams and pictures showing signage requirements etc.
2 Check: General grounds for appeal
Applies to most contraventions.
3 Check: Summarised parking adjudicators decisions
Includes details of example cases won.
sections.contentHtml:
1 Check: Specific grounds for appeal for this contravention (below on this page)
This may also include diagrams and pictures showing signage requirements etc.

2 Check: General grounds for appeal
Applies to most contraventions.

3 Check: Summarised parking adjudicators decisions
Includes details of example cases won.

sections.heading: Specific grounds of appeal for this contravention
sections.content:
1. Was the permit in the windscreen obscured for example by a flyer put under the windscreen wiper or where the windscreen was covered in snow! If so your Penalty charge notice may be cancelled if you send in a photocopy of the permit.
2. Quite often motorists are confused by poor signage, for example where pay and display bays join resident’s only bays. Camden council in London are particularly poor at signposting their various restrictions. If the signage is confusing you should take photographs and make representations. If the signage is not clear and does not comply then the contravention did not occur. Remember that there should be a clear and un-obscured sign within 15 metres (3 car lengths) of where you parked. If you have different types of bays adjoining each other for example pay and display bays adjoining residents parking bays then there should be a sign either at the point where the bays meet with arrows pointing each way and clearly showing the different types of enforcement or signs showing each restriction within 5 meters of each side of the demarcation line. We do not believe it sufficient for there to be just demarcation lines in the Road. See diagrams below.
Below we show diagrams of the signs and bay markings which must be in situ by law and much more information that will help you decide whether to appeal against your penalty charge notice.
1. We hear of penalty charge notices (parking tickets)being issued for this offence even when the pay and display tickets are clearly visible. It may be that you had displayed it in a side window, attached it to the front windscreen or simply placed it on the dash board. Sometimes the adhesive fails and it is possible for a ticket, especially in damp weather, to fall off of the windscreen and land upside down. Sometimes the window fogs up on the inside and the ticket is not visible. If you have a pay and display ticket you should send it in to a local authority confirming it either was displayed clearly or that due to the failure of the adhesive it had fallen off of the wind screen, etc.
3. Were the hours of enforcement clearly shown on the residents permit sign. If not then the hours of enforcement will be the same as the general zone in which you were parked. On entering the zone, were “controlled Zone” entry signs located at each entry point?  Were they facing the right direction?  Were they clearly visible from your car. (ie. Not obstructed, defaced etc.). If not the contravention did not occur as the local authority had not signposted the zone correctly. For more information on controlled zone signs please see our section on contravention code 01 yellow lines.
4. Were the road markings clear? A resident’s permit bay should be clearly shown by having broken white lines marking the restricted bays. They do not have to show individual bays but the lines must be visible.  At the end of the bay(s) there should be single broken bay markings unless the bays adjoin residents bays. Many bays which, for example, adjoin yellow lines have double bay markings at the end which is non compliant.
5. If your parking permit has only recently expired then you should appeal on compassionate grounds, particularly if you are waiting for information from the DVLA, or indeed from the council itself which is required before a permit can be renewed. A number of councils offer a zero tolerance policy so it is a good idea to put the expiry date of your permit in your diary.
Are the resident’s bays enforced 24 hours a day. If so do the signs contain the words “At any time” on them. “At any time” is not an approved variation to the signs and a recent adjudicator’s decision went against the council on this point. You may well wish to appeal on this point. It is possible that the council have a specific permission for the use of these words which they would mention if your appeal is rejected. You can also check with the road signs policy unit at the department of transport.
6. Remember that you may cite compassionate grounds in any appeal or representation. 
Please see our section on parking adjudicators decisions which will have details of cases for this contravention.
 
Below: The required dimensions of an individually marked residents permit holders bay. Please note the permitted variants in the box below the diagram. Obviously if the bay isn’t to the correct dimensions then you may want to appeal. There are no guarantees but the bay should really be compliant in order for it to be enforceable.


Below: A diagram of the sign with the required dimensions. Again check the permitted variants and the sizes of the signs and the letters to see if the signs are compliant.
Back to Parking

Back to Parking
